mappings.rationale: Spans the entire geologic time scale including Precambrian, Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eras and the tectonic and environmental events defining each.
Traces the succession of life through each geological era, from the origin of life in the Precambrian (A) through mass extinctions marking era boundaries.
Examines the fossils characteristic of each geological period, from the Burgess Shale fauna (B.1, Cambrian) through Mesozoic reptiles (C) to Cenozoic mammals.
Directly addresses the dominant fauna of the Mesozoic Era (C), spanning the Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods and the end-Cretaceous extinction.
Mass extinction events define period and era boundaries, including the end-Permian (B.6/C) and end-Cretaceous (C.3/D) transitions.
Provides a comprehensive overview of Earth's geological history through each era, including the physical and chemical changes defining period boundaries.
Directly addresses the Quaternary Period (D.2) and Pleistocene Epoch (D.2.a), including glacial-interglacial cycles and ice sheet dynamics.
Explores glacial periods throughout Earth history including Precambrian glaciations (A), Carboniferous-Permian glaciation (B.5-6), and the Pleistocene (D.2.a).
Illuminates the major evolutionary events defining geological periods, from the Cambrian explosion (B.1) to mammalian radiation in the Cenozoic (D).
The tectonic configuration of continents through geological time provides the paleogeographic framework for understanding each era and period.
Addresses snowball Earth events in the Precambrian (A) and the co-evolution of life and climate through subsequent eras, including greenhouse and icehouse transitions.
Examines the rock formations characteristic of each geological era, from Precambrian igneous and metamorphic rocks (A) to Cenozoic sedimentary sequences (D).
Explores the origin of life and atmospheric oxygen evolution in the Precambrian (A), providing context for the earliest era of geological time.
The evolution and radiation of mammals characterize the Cenozoic Era (D), including their fossil record through the Tertiary and Quaternary periods.
Traces the evolution of Homo and Australopithecus during the Quaternary Period and Pleistocene Epoch (D.2.a).
Explores the Holocene and late Pleistocene (D.2) from the perspective of human prehistory, including the Clovis Culture and Australopithecus.
Reptiles including dinosaurs and crocodilians dominated the Mesozoic Era (C) across the Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods.
Amphibian evolution and the transition to land are central to the Devonian and Carboniferous periods (B.4-B.5) when vertebrates first colonized terrestrial habitats.
Reef-building corals are key organisms of Paleozoic and Mesozoic marine ecosystems (B-C), marking important biostratigraphic intervals.
Explores the geomorphological processes and landforms shaped during the Quaternary Period (D.2), including glacial landscapes and sea level changes.
